items.title: Australian women, children linked to ISIS return from Syria
items.summary_auto: Airliners carrying 13 Australian women and children with alleged ties to the Islamic State group landed in on Thursday in their homeland, where the adults face potential criminal charges relating to their alleged time in the extremist organization's so-called caliphate, which spanned Syria and Iraq.

items.title: N.L. man set to take stand in trial for 1st-degree murder of his estranged wife
Contact tracing underway for dozens who got off cruise ship amid hantavirus outbreak
items.summary_auto: Countries worldwide scrambled on Thursday to trace people who had left the ‌cruise ship hit by a deadly hantavirus outbreak before it was anchored off the coast of Cape Verde to prevent further spread of the disease, which has so far killed three people. Dozens disembarked in St. Helena before the outbreak was reported, it was learned on Thursday.

items.title: 'Very gentle soul': Family, friends devastated after Métis woman, 77, killed in Saskatoon
items.summary_auto: On a day dedicated to honouring missing and murdered Indigenous women and girls, family and friends of Dory Cook had very fresh grief in their hearts.

items.title: Poll finds many Albertans want province to have greater say in who immigrates
items.summary_auto: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith plans to hold a referendum in October and ask several questions about immigration. To get an early sense of how Albertans are feeling, CBC News included two related questions in a poll by Janet Brown Opinion Research last month.

items.title: In the days before she died, no one called for medical help. Her family wants change
items.summary_auto: Johanna Sutcliffe's family is critical of the care she received in her final days, and they’re calling for systemic change to close what they see as a gap in provincial standards of care at special care homes.

items.title: N.S. care workers say company scrambling to replace staff as long-standing workers leave
items.summary_auto: Workers at a private company the Nova Scotia government contracts to care for deeply vulnerable people say Arden Professional Client Care is scrambling to replace some of its most experienced staff.

items.title: Care workers say company scrambling to replace staff as long-standing workers leave
items.summary_auto: Workers at a private company the Nova Scotia government contracts to care for deeply vulnerable people say Arden Professional Client Care is scrambling to replace some of its most experienced staff.

items.title: The drone war comes home: Canada scrambles to shield military bases in legal grey zone
items.summary_auto: Canada’s military is quietly deploying counter-drone defences at ports and air bases, racing to keep pace with lessons from Ukraine’s devastating strikes on Russian assets. The effort exposes a legal and operational grey zone — how to stop airborne threats over Canadian cities without disrupting civilian life or crossing regulatory lines.

items.title: These students are limiting their own smartphone, social media and tech use. Here's why
items.summary_auto: Some young Canadians are getting more intentional with their technology use and opting for digital minimalism — a paring down of all-encompassing devices like smartphones — of their own volition.

items.title: Quebec family mourns after 911 calls for father's heart attack misrouted to Ontario
items.summary_auto: A man in Quebec's Abitibi-Témiscamingue region is questioning if his father would still be alive if his family's 911 calls were properly transferred to Quebec, instead of landing in Ontario. By the time paramedics arrived, roughly 50 minutes had passed from the first 911 call.

items.title: Mexico trade mission launches in Toronto as businesses seek to expand ties with Canada
items.summary_auto: A major Mexican trade mission to Canada launched Thursday, involving over 200 Mexican businesses seeking to expand commercial links during the two-day, two-city encounter that will include high-level talks on the Canada-US-Mexico Agreement.

items.title: Canadian government to pay $8.7M to settle data breach class-action involving CRA accounts
items.summary_auto: The federal government will pay $8.7 million to settle a class-action lawsuit involving tens of thousands of Canadians whose sensitive information was compromised or stolen when hackers got into their accounts on government websites, including the Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) portal.

items.title: 5 years after Quebec law, 129 Indigenous families still need answers on missing children
items.summary_auto: According to a recent government report, 129 Indigenous families have started the process of searching for 221 missing children since the law, known as Bill 79, came into effect.

items.title: Why disarming Hezbollah is about much more than guns and rockets
items.summary_auto: Facing an existential breaking point, Lebanon’s government is attempting to disarm Hezbollah and assert state sovereignty following a fragile, often-violated April ceasefire with Israel. The path forward remains a dangerous gamble.

items.title: Children's aid system faces heightened calls for change after Ontario couple's murder, torture convictions
items.summary_auto: The judge in the trial for an Ontario couple who'd been caring for two brothers was clear in laying blame: Becky Hamber and Brandy Cooney are the ones who committed murder and other crimes. He also noted that the Children's Aid Society (CAS) had "zero to do" with their guilt or innocence. Still, the case raises concerns about the child protection system and has showcased systemic issues that need to be addressed immediately, say advocates and other observers.

items.title: Advocates slam Health Canada's new voluntary breast implant registry, MP 'deeply disappointed'
items.summary_auto: Health Canada has launched a new voluntary registry that will allow patients and health-care professionals to receive direct alerts about breast implant recalls and safety issues, but some patient advocates and medical professionals say it doesn't go far enough.

items.title: 1st wave of free FIFA Fan Festival tickets in Toronto snapped up in 4 hours
items.summary_auto: The first wave of free tickets to the FIFA Fan Festival in Toronto in June were snapped up on Wednesday within four hours of being made available online, an official says.

items.title: Japan just invented a new word for ‘cruel heat’
items.summary_auto: Temperatures in Japan have reached a breaking point, and the language is shifting to keep up. The Japan Meteorological Agency has introduced a new term for days exceeding 40 C: kokushobi, or "cruelly hot day." In a culture with a deep relationship between seasons and language, CBC News's Johanna Wagstaffe explores how the arrival of this word is a sobering signal of climate change.

items.title: B.C.-based group planning bid for Vancouver Whitecaps, minister says
items.summary_auto: British Columbia Jobs Minister Ravi Kahlon says the provincial government has been approached by a local group that wants to buy the Vancouver Whitecaps.

items.title: Advocate to return 'disingenuous' award after mayor's opposition to Vancouver overdose prevention site
items.summary_auto: A prominent harm reduction and recovery advocate says he's returning an honour bestowed upon him by Vancouver's mayor amid a political battle over a proposed overdose prevention site (OPS) in the city centre.

items.title: Grey whale struck by Sea-Doo in Vancouver appears uninjured, 'acting normal,' says researcher
items.summary_auto: The Department of Fisheries and Oceans was able to assess the animal for injuries on Tuesday.

items.title: Former NHL goalie Carey Price among those calling for changes to Indian Act
Alberta voters list leak puts domestic violence victims on edge, organizations say
items.summary_auto: It's unknown how many people may have been able to access an online database with the personal information of millions of Albertans. The CEO of a domestic violence organization in Calgary calls the situation "disturbing" and says it puts victims at risk.

items.title: Hiker charged by grizzly bear near popular trail in Kananaskis Country
items.summary_auto: A hiker had a close call with a charging grizzly bear near the the popular hiking spot of Troll Falls in Kananaskis Country, according to a provincial warning.

items.title: Airbus secures 150-plane order with AirAsia in multi-billion dollar boon for Quebec aviation
Anthropic AI says deal struck to use SpaceX data centres
items.summary_auto: Anthropic on Wednesday said it reached a deal to tap the computing resources of Elon Musk's SpaceX, marking a détente with its one-time critic and ‌a boost for both companies in the high-stakes artificial intelligence race.

items.title: Carney to 'take into account' advice on Senate appointments from Trudeau-era committee
items.summary_auto: Prime Minister Mark Carney said Wednesday he will "take into account" the advice of an independent advisory body to fill a number of vacancies in the Senate — though he didn't say explicitly how that will impact his decisions on appointments.

items.title: Quebec pharmacy chain pulls energy drinks as pressure mounts on government for youth ban
items.summary_auto: A Quebec City-based pharmacy chain says it will remove energy drinks from its store shelves amid growing calls for the Quebec government to ban the sale of the high-caffeine beverages to children.

items.title: Millennials more likely to live with their parents than past generations: StatsCan report
items.summary_auto: Analysis of data from three census cycles showed that 16.3 per cent of millennials aged 25 to 39 were living with parents in 2021, whereas 8.2 per cent of baby boomers in 1991 were doing the same. But the high cost of housing wasn't entirely to blame, according to Statistics Canada.

items.title: Honda more definitively halting $15B EV plant development in Ontario, according to reports
items.summary_auto: Japanese news media reported that Honda Motor plans to indefinitely halt the development of its $15-billion electric vehicle complex in Canada, though Honda Canada said they had nothing to report at this time. Ottawa acknowledged automakers face "significant challenges," but did not say whether the company had updated them on those plans.

items.title: Why the Lebanon-Israel ceasefire is 'in name only'
items.summary_auto: Nearly three weeks into a U.S.-brokered ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel, escalating attacks and widening incursions are exposing major cracks in an agreement that analysts say was never going to ultimately halt the violence.

items.title: Alaska landslide set off CN Tower-sized tsunami last year — and a warning for B.C.
items.summary_auto: When millions of tonnes of rock fell one kilometre into an Alaskan fiord last year, it set off one of the largest tsunamis ever recorded, a monstrous 481-metre wave higher than the tallest viewing platform of the CN Tower, a new study shows.

items.title: Russia fires dozens of drones at Ukraine in 'obvious spurning' of ceasefire, says Zelenskyy
items.summary_auto: Russia fired dozens of drones at Ukraine in nighttime attacks, Ukrainian officials said Wednesday, disregarding a unilateral ceasefire announced by Kyiv that began at midnight.
